section.title: Praise the Lord, All Nations
section.sectionRationale: The shortest chapter in the entire Bible, this Hallel psalm packs a universal missionary vision into two verses, summoning every nation and people to praise the LORD, a summons Paul quotes to defend the inclusion of the Gentiles.
section.thesis: God's covenant love toward Israel is the very ground on which every nation on earth is summoned to praise Him, because His faithfulness was never meant to stay within one people's borders.
section.frameNote: A single, tightly compressed movement in two verses: a universal call to praise, verse 1, grounded in the greatness of God's loving devotion and the eternity of His faithfulness, verse 2.

units.label: Praise the LORD, all you nations
units.note: the shortest chapter, the widest summons

verses.text: Praise the LORD, all you nations! Extol Him, all you peoples!
For great is His {{chesed|loving devotion}} toward us, and the faithfulness of the LORD endures forever. Hallelujah!

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant love and loyalty. That this love shown specifically toward Israel, 'us,' verse 2, becomes the stated reason for all nations to praise God shows His covenant faithfulness was always intended to overflow to the whole world.

basic.context: In just two verses, the shortest chapter in Scripture, this psalm summons every nation and people on earth to praise the LORD, grounding that universal call in His steadfast covenant love and enduring faithfulness shown specifically to Israel.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's brevity does not limit its scope; rather, its compressed form intensifies a sweeping missionary vision that Paul later reads as proof that God always intended the Gentiles to join Israel in praising Him, Romans 15:11.
basic.originalLanguages: The basis for universal praise is God's chesed, His steadfast covenant love, shown toward 'us,' meaning Israel, verse 2. That love shown to one people becomes, in this psalm's own logic, the reason all peoples everywhere are summoned to praise Him.
basic.typology: This psalm's summons anticipates the promise to Abraham that in his offspring all the families of the earth would be blessed, Genesis 12:3, a promise fulfilled when the ultimate offspring of Abraham, Christ, becomes a light to the Gentiles as well as the glory of His people Israel, Luke 2:32.
basic.christ: Paul quotes this psalm directly in Romans 15:11 as part of his argument that Christ's coming was always intended to bring the Gentiles to glorify God for His mercy, alongside Israel. The chesed shown to Israel that grounds this psalm's universal call is fulfilled in Christ, the offspring of Abraham, through whom God's covenant faithfulness reaches every nation and people exactly as this two-verse psalm anticipates.
basic.god: God's covenant love is not tribal or exclusive in its ultimate purpose; it is shown concretely to Israel yet is so great and so enduring that it becomes grounds for summoning literally every nation and people on earth to praise Him.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 12:3 promises that all the families of the earth will be blessed through Abraham's offspring. Romans 15:11 quotes this psalm directly to defend the full inclusion of Gentile believers in praising God. Revelation 7:9-10 pictures the psalm's summons finally and fully answered, a great multitude from every nation praising God and the Lamb.
basic.oneStory: God's steadfast love shown to Israel was never meant to remain within one people's borders; this brief but sweeping psalm summons every nation to praise Him on that very basis, a summons Paul reads as fulfilled in Christ, through whom God's covenant faithfulness at last reaches, and will finally be answered by, every nation and every people on earth.

context.literary: The shortest chapter in the Bible, just two verses and seventeen Hebrew words, framed by an inclusio, Praise the LORD at the start and Hallelujah at the close, with tight synonymous parallelism, nations and peoples, loving devotion and faithfulness, packing maximal scope into minimal space.
context.historical: As one of the Egyptian Hallel psalms, 113-118, sung at Passover, Tabernacles, and other feasts, this brief hymn was likely composed after a specific deliverance recognized as evidence of God's mercy, yet its brevity and universal address make it timeless and endlessly repeatable.
context.canonical: Standing between the personal thanksgiving of Psalm 116 and the climactic celebration of Psalm 118, this psalm briefly but decisively widens the Hallel's scope from Israel's own experience to the whole world's obligation to praise the same God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon every nation and people, without exception, to praise the LORD, grounding this universal call in the concrete covenant love and faithfulness He has already shown to His own people Israel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm's summons is itself a standing command, not merely a description of a past event; it prescribes ongoing, universal praise as the fitting response to God's covenant love and abiding faithfulness, for every nation across every age.

debates.question: Does this psalm's universal address to 'all nations' anticipate an already-present Old Testament reality, or does it primarily look forward prophetically to a future inclusion of the Gentiles?

views.label: The psalm addresses Gentile nations who could already witness and respond to Israel's God through general revelation and Israel's own testimony
views.case: Passages like the Queen of Sheba's visit, 1 Kings 10, and Rahab's confession, Joshua 2:11, show Gentiles in the Old Testament era already capable of recognizing and praising Israel's God, so this summons need not be understood as purely futuristic.

views.label: The psalm functions prophetically, anticipating a future, fuller inclusion of the nations only realized through the gospel
views.case: Paul specifically cites this psalm in Romans 15:11 as scriptural proof that God always planned Gentile inclusion through Christ, suggesting its truest and fullest referent is the church age, when the nations are actually gathered in through the gospel rather than remaining occasional individual converts.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that God's concern for the nations was present in the Old Testament and reaches full flower in the New Testament, when the gospel of Christ actually gathers believers from every nation to fulfill this psalm's summons.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's ground for universal praise is God's chesed, His steadfast covenant love, shown specifically toward 'us,' meaning Israel, verse 2. That particular, concrete love becomes, remarkably, the very reason every nation and people on earth is summoned to praise Him.

typology.type: The promise that all families of the earth would be blessed through Abraham's offspring, Genesis 12:3
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, Abraham's ultimate offspring, a light for revelation to the Gentiles and for glory to His people Israel, Luke 2:32.

typology.type: The universal summons to praise, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and fully answered in the vision of a great multitude from every nation, tribe, people, and tongue praising God and the Lamb, Revelation 7:9-10.

study.christ: Paul quotes this very psalm directly, Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles, and sing praises to Him, all you peoples, Romans 15:11, as scriptural proof that Christ's coming was always intended to bring Gentiles to glorify God for His mercy alongside believing Israel. The chesed shown to Israel that grounds this psalm's universal call reaches its fulfillment in Christ, Abraham's promised offspring, through whom the blessing promised to all nations, Genesis 12:3, is finally and truly realized.
study.god: God's covenant love, though shown concretely to Israel, is never meant to remain confined to one people; it is so great and so enduring that it becomes, in itself, the reason every nation and people on earth is summoned to praise Him.

crossReferences.note: Promises that all the families of the earth will be blessed through Abraham's offspring, the seed promise this psalm's universal summons anticipates.
Paul quotes this psalm directly as proof that God always intended the Gentiles to praise Him alongside Israel through Christ.
Pictures this psalm's summons finally and fully answered by a great multitude from every nation praising God and the Lamb.

study.oneStory: God's steadfast love shown concretely to Israel was never meant to remain within one people's borders; this shortest of all psalms summons every nation to praise Him on that very basis, a summons Paul reads as fulfilled in Christ, the promised offspring of Abraham, through whom God's covenant faithfulness reaches, and one day will be fully answered by, every nation and people on earth.

sermon.title: Everlasting Truth for the Joy of All Peoples
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly on Psalm 117, unpacking who the nations and peoples are, God's purpose that all peoples praise Him, and how the basis of that praise, God's steadfast love and faithfulness toward Israel, leads inevitably to Jesus Christ as the one through whom all nations are blessed.

quiz.q: How many verses does Psalm 117 contain, and what is notable about this?

options.text: Two verses, making it the shortest chapter in the entire Bible.
options.note: Its brevity does not limit its scope; it carries one of Scripture's most sweeping missionary visions.

options.text: Twenty-nine verses, making it one of the longest psalms.
options.note: That verse count describes Psalm 118, not Psalm 117, which is famously the shortest chapter in Scripture.

options.text: Ten verses, matching the length of Psalm 111.
options.note: Psalm 117 has only two verses, far shorter than Psalm 111's ten.

options.text: Seven verses, matching the length of Psalm 110.
options.note: Psalm 117 has only two verses, shorter even than Psalm 110's seven.

quiz.q: Who is summoned to praise the LORD in verse 1?

options.text: All nations and all peoples.
options.note: This universal address extends the call to praise far beyond Israel's own borders.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The summons is explicitly universal, addressing all nations and peoples, not merely priests.

options.text: Only the king and his royal officials.
options.note: The call is addressed to all nations and peoples broadly, not a narrow royal circle.

options.text: Only the descendants of Abraham by blood.
options.note: The summons explicitly extends to all nations, not solely Abraham's physical descendants.

quiz.q: What is given in verse 2 as the reason all nations should praise the LORD?

options.text: His great loving devotion toward us and His faithfulness enduring forever.
options.note: God's covenant love shown to Israel becomes the stated basis for universal praise.

options.text: Israel's military conquest of surrounding nations.
options.note: The reason given is God's loving devotion and faithfulness, not a military conquest.

options.text: The wealth and splendor of Solomon's temple.
options.note: The psalm's reasoning centers on God's character, His love and faithfulness, not temple architecture.

options.text: A treaty signed between Israel and its neighbors.
options.note: The basis given is theological, God's covenant love and faithfulness, not a political treaty.

quiz.q: How does Paul use this psalm in Romans 15:11?

options.text: He quotes it as scriptural proof that God always intended Gentiles to praise Him alongside Israel through Christ.
options.note: This is one of several Old Testament texts Paul cites to defend full Gentile inclusion in the gospel.

options.text: He quotes it to argue against including any Gentiles in the church.
options.note: Paul's use is the opposite; he cites it in defense of full Gentile inclusion.

options.text: He quotes it only in reference to the Roman emperor's authority.
options.note: Paul's application concerns the praise of God by all nations through the gospel, not imperial authority.

options.text: He quotes it to predict a specific future date for Christ's return.
options.note: The quotation supports Gentile inclusion in praising God now, not a chronological prediction.

quiz.q: What promise to Abraham does this psalm's universal summons anticipate?

options.text: That in Abraham's offspring, all the families of the earth would be blessed, Genesis 12:3.
options.note: This Abrahamic promise is the seed anticipation that this psalm's universal call to praise later flowers into.

options.text: That Abraham's descendants would remain a small, isolated people forever.
options.note: The Abrahamic promise explicitly includes blessing for all nations, the opposite of isolation.

options.text: That Abraham would receive great personal wealth alone.
options.note: The relevant promise concerns blessing for all nations, not merely Abraham's personal wealth.

options.text: That Abraham's descendants would conquer all surrounding empires militarily.
options.note: The promise anticipated is one of blessing extended to all nations, not military conquest.

questions: This shortest of all psalms carries one of the widest visions in Scripture, that every nation would praise God. Does the size of your own current prayers and praise match the size of God's actual purposes for the world?

The basis for universal praise is God's covenant love shown specifically to Israel. How does seeing God's faithfulness to one people as the ground for blessing all peoples change the way you think about His faithfulness to you personally?

Paul reads this psalm as proof that God always intended the Gentiles to be included in praising Him through Christ. Where do you need to expand your own vision of who belongs at the table of God's people?

This psalm anticipates a day, pictured in Revelation 7, when a multitude from every nation will praise God together. How might you take one practical step this week toward that vision, whether through prayer, giving, or relationship?

Piper notes that missions is not arrogance but love, calling the nations to the joy they were created for. Who is one person or people group you could pray for this week to know and praise this same God?

translationNotes.note: Peoples renders the Hebrew ummim, a term for distinct ethnic or cultural groups, paired with goyim, nations, together expressing the widest possible scope of humanity, every group however defined.
This verse is quoted directly by Paul in Romans 15:11 in defense of the full inclusion of Gentile believers alongside Jewish believers in praising God through Christ.
Loving devotion renders chesed, God's steadfast covenant love shown concretely to Israel, here presented as the very basis for a universal summons extending far beyond Israel's own borders.
